nodes.statement: Over 4 million children live in low-income families in the UK, with the two-child benefit limit affecting 483,000 families and 1.67 million children since its 2015 introduction
The DWP estimates 300,000 children are in relative poverty directly as a result of the two-child limit, equivalent to approximately 100 children pushed into poverty every day since introduction
DWP modelling shows removing the two-child limit would lift 450,000 children out of relative poverty by end of parliament and increase household income for 2 million children
2.8 million people are economically inactive due to long-term sickness, up 800,000 since pre-pandemic levels, with the NHS waiting list of 7.6 million a direct driver of inability to work
60% of children in poverty live in working households, meaning in-work poverty driven by low wages, insecure contracts, high housing costs, and inadequate childcare is the dominant form of poverty

The two-child limit increased employment of affected mothers by approximately 3 percentage points from a 50% baseline, but the substantial majority of mothers did not respond through employment and had lower income
Affected households lose £4,300 per year on average from the two-child limit, representing approximately 10% of household income
Children growing up in poverty earn approximately 25% less at age 30 than their non-poor peers, creating a measurable lifetime economic cost
Unpaid carers save the state an estimated £162 billion per year, more than total NHS spending, while having worse health outcomes and higher poverty rates than non-carers
Non-take-up of means-tested benefits is estimated at £10-15 billion per year, meaning people in poverty are not claiming what they are entitled to because the system is too complex
The Scottish Child Payment of £26.70 per week per child for low-income families has demonstrably reduced child poverty in Scotland relative to the rest of the UK
The UC taper rate means some workers face effective marginal tax rates above 60%, creating a poverty trap where additional earnings are largely clawed back
The two-child benefit limit reduces poverty or incentivises work sufficiently to justify its impact on children
nodes.verdict: REMOVAL IS CORRECT. DWP's own analysis: removal lifts 450,000 children from poverty. Employment effect is 3 percentage points — meaningful but the substantial majority of mothers don't respond. The policy trades mass child poverty for a marginal work incentive.

nodes.statement: £12 billion in welfare savings can be achieved through tightening eligibility and increased sanctions as the Conservatives proposed
nodes.verdict: NOT CREDIBLE. IFS says it looks 'difficult in the extreme'. Disability rises are driven by genuine long-term sickness up 800,000. Sanctions increase destitution without reliably increasing employment. Tightening eligibility shifts costs to NHS, homelessness, criminal justice.

nodes.statement: Universal Basic Income is feasible at meaningful levels in the UK
nodes.verdict: THEORETICALLY ATTRACTIVE, PRACTICALLY CHALLENGING. Finland pilot: modest wellbeing gains, no employment effect. Full UBI at £10k/yr would cost ~£500bn — equal to total government spending. Partial UBI (guaranteed minimum) is more feasible but functionally similar to UC with less conditionality. Pilot worth exploring.

nodes.statement: Getting people into work is sufficient to solve poverty in the UK
nodes.verdict: WORK ALONE IS INSUFFICIENT. 60% of children in poverty live in working households. In-work poverty is the dominant form. Low wages, zero-hours contracts, high housing costs, and inadequate childcare mean employment alone doesn't prevent poverty. The welfare system must address in-work poverty not just worklessness.

nodes.statement: The rise in disability benefit claims is driven by fraud rather than genuine increases in long-term sickness
nodes.verdict: GENUINE NEED. The 800,000 increase since the pandemic is driven by mental health, musculoskeletal problems, and long Covid. DWP data shows fraud is a small fraction. NHS waiting lists prevent treatment that would enable return to work. Address the health causes, not the benefit eligibility.

nodes.statement: The benefit cap effectively reduces poverty or welfare dependency rather than simply shifting costs elsewhere
nodes.verdict: THE BENEFIT CAP ADDRESSES A SYMPTOM NOT A CAUSE. In high-cost areas the cap forces families into cheaper housing, disrupting education and employment. The cap doesn't reduce housing costs — the underlying problem is housing cost, not benefit generosity.

nodes.statement: The welfare budget is rising not because of generosity but because every other system is failing: housing costs drive housing benefit, NHS waiting lists drive sickness benefits, low wages drive in-work poverty

The two-child limit is the clearest single policy failure in the entire manifesto, with the government's own analysis showing it pushes 100 children into poverty every day for a marginal 3 percentage point employment effect
Welfare is a downstream node that rises when upstream systems fail — it cannot be fixed by cutting welfare spending, only by fixing the upstream failures in NHS, housing, education, and energy
Complete removal of the two-child benefit limit, lifting an estimated 450,000 children from poverty at a cost of £2.4-3.2 billion per year

Establish an independent commission on Universal Credit adequacy to recommend annual uprates based on the JRF Minimum Income Standard, closing the £35-50 per week gap between UC rates and actual cost of essentials
Implement automatic benefits enrolment using HMRC and DWP data to reverse the default, recovering the estimated £10-15 billion per year in unclaimed means-tested benefits
Adopt the Scottish Child Payment model for England with direct cash payments per child for low-income families, proven to reduce child poverty at modest cost
Deliver a carers' recognition package including £2 per hour minimum wage premium, guaranteed respite, pension credit for caring, and caring as a protected characteristic
Treat long-term sickness as a joint NHS-DWP economic emergency with dedicated return-to-work rehabilitation, mental health provision, and Access to Work backlog clearance
Reform the UC taper rate to reduce effective marginal tax rates below 60% for low-income workers, eliminating the poverty trap where additional earnings are largely clawed back

Maintain the two-child limit, achieve £12 billion in welfare savings through tightened disability benefits and increased sanctions, and reduce welfare rolls through conditionality while protecting the pension triple lock

Remove the two-child limit from April 2026 via legislation, develop a child poverty strategy, review Universal Credit, and launch the Get Britain Working plan tackling NHS waits, mental health, and Access to Work alongside a Fair Pay Agreement for social care

Remove the two-child limit and benefit cap, end deep poverty within a decade, establish an independent UC adequacy commission, introduce a carer's minimum wage at £2 per hour above standard, and expand Carer's Allowance eligibility

Pilot Universal Basic Income, remove all benefit caps and limits, restore benefits to pre-austerity levels, end the sanctions regime entirely, and fund £20 billion in free personal care through wealth taxes

Cut £50 billion in government spending, incentivise employment through tax cuts rather than welfare reform, redirect overseas aid to domestic welfare, and prioritise British citizens for social housing and benefits
